does: This paper releases an open source implementation of a model organism framework previously developed for studying auditing games. The replication provides a computational tool that researchers can access, modify, and extend to investigate how auditing mechanisms function under various conditions. By making the code and experimental setup publicly available, the work enables reproducible research and lowers barriers for other scientists to build upon the auditing game framework without needing to re-implement the core infrastructure from scratch.
connects: This work builds directly on prior research into auditing games, a theoretical and computational framework for studying the interaction between auditors and auditees in strategic settings. It extends the tradition of using model organisms and simplified systems to understand complex real-world phenomena, a practice established across computational social science and multi-agent systems research. The open source release connects to the broader movement toward reproducibility and open science in machine learning and AI research, responding to recognized challenges in replicating complex algorithmic and game-theoretic work.
enables: By providing accessible infrastructure, this replication enables future research to rapidly prototype variations of auditing game mechanisms, test novel auditing strategies, and investigate how different rule structures affect strategic behavior. The open source availability supports comparative studies across auditing frameworks and could facilitate empirical validation of theoretical predictions. This foundation enables researchers to explore applications of auditing game insights to real-world domains like regulatory compliance, AI safety monitoring, and adversarial robustness testing, while also supporting educational use and broader community engagement with game-theoretic modeling.
